_meta.description: Per-occurrence contextual glosses for theologically loaded NT lemmas. Keyed by `<book-slug>:<chapter>:<verse>:<lemma>`. Pilot set authored by hand, in the style of the hand-curated James glosses. Phase 3 will expand this via Claude API batch generation against the loaded-lemma list.
_meta.format: string with markdown not allowed; one to three sentences; phrasing 'In X, the word foregrounds Y' — never 'X means Y'.

john:1:1:logos: ὁ λόγος here is the divine self-expression — the Word who was 'with God' and 'was God,' the Son who utters the Father. Not abstract reason or message; a Person.
john:1:14:logos: ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο — 'the Word became flesh.' The same divine self-expression of v.1 now takes σάρξ, full human creatureliness. The verse is the hinge of Johannine Christology.
hebrews:4:12:logos: ζῶν γὰρ ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ — 'the word of God is living.' Here ὁ λόγος is the spoken word of God in scripture, personified as an active agent: it cuts, divides, exposes. Closer to a sword in God's hand than to abstract truth.
john:3:16:kosmos: ὁ κόσμος here is humanity — the world of persons God loves. The same word in 1 Jn 2:15 ('do not love the κόσμος') means the world-system in rebellion against God. Same lexeme, opposite valence; context governs which.
1-john:2:15:kosmos: Not the κόσμος God loved in Jn 3:16 (humanity) but the world-system arrayed against God — its values, its allegiances, its grip on the soul. The same word, deliberately split between Johannine writings.
romans:5:8:agape: ἀγάπη here is God's settled covenant commitment, demonstrated in action — Christ's death FOR sinners, not for the righteous. The English 'love' under-translates the verbal force: love that does, not love that feels.
1-corinthians:13:4:agape: Paul's famous ἀγάπη clauses are not feelings but actions: μακροθυμεῖ (waits long with people), χρηστεύεται (gives kindness), οὐ ζηλοῖ (does not envy). ἀγάπη here is described entirely by what it does and does not do — a defining test, not a feeling.
john:13:34:agape: καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς — 'as I have loved you.' The standard for ἀγάπη here is Christ's own act of self-giving; the disciples' love for one another is measured against the cross.
ephesians:2:8:charis: τῇ γὰρ χάριτί ἐστε σεσῳσμένοι — 'by χάρις you have been saved.' Here χάρις is God's unmerited initiative concretized in Christ's death and resurrection. Not abstract favor — the saving act itself, received through πίστις.
2-corinthians:12:9:charis: ἀρκεῖ σοι ἡ χάρις μου — 'my χάρις is sufficient for you.' In Paul's weakness, χάρις is God's enabling power flowing in answer to limitation, not a substance Paul possesses.
romans:5:15:charis: ἡ χάρις τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ἡ δωρεὰ ἐν χάριτι — Paul stacks χάρις with δωρεά ('gift') to emphasize gratuity. Adam's trespass spread to all; God's χάρις in Christ spreads further.
romans:1:17:dikaiosune: δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ here is the saving righteousness God gives to faith — the right standing that becomes the believer's. Pauline forensic register. The verse opens Romans' main argument: God's righteousness is revealed in the gospel, received ἐκ πίστεως εἰς πίστιν.
romans:3:21:dikaiosune: νυνὶ δὲ χωρὶς νόμου δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ πεφανέρωται — 'now apart from law, God's δικαιοσύνη has been disclosed.' The same word in Mt 5:6 ('hunger and thirst for righteousness') keeps the ethical register; here Paul means God's saving verdict.
philippians:3:9:dikaiosune: μὴ ἔχων ἐμὴν δικαιοσύνην — 'not having a δικαιοσύνη of my own.' Paul contrasts his own righteousness (ἐκ νόμου) with the righteousness from God (ἐκ θεοῦ ἐπὶ τῇ πίστει). Personal language, but the forensic logic of Rom 3 stands behind it.
romans:7:18:sarx: οὐκ οἰκεῖ ἐν ἐμοί, τοῦτ' ἔστιν ἐν τῇ σαρκί μου, ἀγαθόν — 'nothing good dwells in me, that is, in my σάρξ.' σάρξ here is not the body simpliciter but humanity-as-fallen, the self under sin's sway. Don't read this as anti-body.
romans:8:5:sarx: οἱ κατὰ σάρκα ὄντες — 'those who are according to σάρξ.' Paul means a settled orientation — to live by fallen human nature's logic — contrasted with κατὰ πνεῦμα. σάρξ here is mode of existence, not flesh-and-bone.
galatians:5:16:sarx: ἐπιθυμίαν σαρκὸς οὐ μὴ τελέσητε — the σάρξ wars against the πνεῦμα in the believer. Same Pauline anthropology as Rom 7-8: σάρξ as fallen human nature still operative until glorification, not as physicality.
hebrews:11:1:pistis: ἔστιν δὲ πίστις ἐλπιζομένων ὑπόστασις — πίστις is the substance (ὑπόστασις) of things hoped for. The author defines πίστις not by what it feels like but by what it grasps: future realities made present through trust in God's promise.
romans:4:3:pistis: ἐπίστευσεν Ἀβραάμ — Abraham trusted, and it was reckoned to him as δικαιοσύνη. πίστις here is the relational act of taking God at his word, the model Paul will extend to all who 'trust the one who justifies the ungodly.'
galatians:3:11:pistis: ὁ δίκαιος ἐκ πίστεως ζήσεται — quoting Habakkuk. πίστις here is the believer's stance toward God, not a doctrinal content. The line drives the whole 'works of law vs. faith' argument in Galatians.
matthew:6:33:basileia: ζητεῖτε δὲ πρῶτον τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ — 'seek first the βασιλεία of God.' Not a place but God's active reign — his saving rule breaking into history. Jesus is preaching its arrival; the call is to align life with its priorities.
mark:1:15:basileia: ἤγγικεν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ — 'the βασιλεία of God has drawn near.' The verb perfect — it has approached and remains near. Jesus' opening proclamation defines the rest of Mark.
john:3:8:pneuma: τὸ πνεῦμα ὅπου θέλει πνεῖ — Jesus puns: πνεῦμα means wind AND Spirit. The Spirit blows freely, like wind; you hear the sound but cannot see the source. Birth ἐκ πνεύματος (v.5) has the same untraceable freedom.
romans:8:9:pneuma: εἰ δέ τις πνεῦμα Χριστοῦ οὐκ ἔχει — Paul interchanges 'Spirit of God,' 'Spirit of Christ,' and 'Christ in you' across three verses. πνεῦμα here is the indwelling life of the risen Christ, the structural mark of belonging to him.
romans:1:16:soteria: δύναμις γὰρ θεοῦ ἐστιν εἰς σωτηρίαν — 'the power of God for σωτηρία.' Not narrowly future heaven but the comprehensive saving act of God in Christ — past (cross), present (justification, sanctification), future (glory). The gospel announces it.
galatians:3:24:nomos: ὁ νόμος παιδαγωγὸς ἡμῶν γέγονεν — 'the νόμος has become our παιδαγωγός (guardian) until Christ.' Paul restages Torah as the household disciplinarian who escorts the heir to maturity. Not abolished — fulfilled and out of role.
romans:7:7:nomos: ὁ νόμος ἁμαρτία; μὴ γένοιτο. ἀλλὰ τὴν ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἔγνων εἰ μὴ διὰ νόμου — the νόμος exposes ἁμαρτία; it does not produce it. Paul defends Torah's holiness even as he diagnoses its inability to save.
genesis:1:1:bara: Scripture's opening verb. בָּרָא in the Qal takes ONLY God as subject — a unique grammatical reservation marking creation as divine prerogative. The verse establishes the whole biblical theology of God as Maker.
genesis:1:2:ruach: וְרוּחַ אֱלֹהִים מְרַחֶפֶת — 'and the רוּחַ of God was hovering.' The verb (rachaph) is used elsewhere only of a mother bird brooding over her young (Deut 32:11). Spirit-as-wind / Spirit-as-breath / Spirit-as-living-presence are all present in this first chapter.
genesis:1:26:tselem: נַעֲשֶׂה אָדָם בְּצַלְמֵנוּ — 'let us make humanity in our צֶלֶם.' צֶלֶם means a representative image, like a king's statue stationed in his territories to mark his rule. Humanity images God by ruling-on-behalf — a vocation, not an attribute.
genesis:1:27:bara: Used three times in Gen 1: cosmic creation (v.1), animal life (v.21), and humanity (v.27). The verse stacks בָּרָא three times in one breath to mark humanity's creation as a distinct creative threshold.
genesis:2:7:neshamah: וַיִּפַּח בְּאַפָּיו נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים — 'he breathed into his nostrils the נְשָׁמָה of life.' Different word from רוּחַ but adjacent: נְשָׁמָה is the breath specifically as life-gift, what makes adam a נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה ('living being').
genesis:3:15:zera: וְאֵיבָה אָשִׁית בֵּינְךָ וּבֵין הָאִשָּׁה וּבֵין זַרְעֲךָ וּבֵין זַרְעָהּ — the protoevangelium. Two זֶרַע lines in conflict, the woman's seed crushing the serpent's head. Christian reading takes this as the first messianic promise; Paul reads Gal 3:16 through the singular ('seed' = Christ).
genesis:12:3:barakh: וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה — 'in you all the families of the earth shall be בָּרַךְ (blessed).' The Abrahamic promise that universalizes Israel's election: blessing flows out, not stops with the chosen.
genesis:15:6:emet: וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה — 'and Abram אָמַן (believed/trusted) the LORD.' Same Hebrew root as אֱמֶת (truth, firmness). To trust YHWH is to lean one's weight on the One who stands firm. Paul (Rom 4) and James (Jas 2) both read this verse — to different ends.
genesis:22:2:olah: וְהַעֲלֵהוּ שָׁם לְעֹלָה — 'offer him there as a עֹלָה.' The עֹלָה is the whole-burnt offering, entirely consumed on the altar — nothing kept back. The Akedah's terrible logic: God asks Abraham to give back the very son He gave.
genesis:22:14:yehovah: וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם שֵׁם־הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא יְהוָה יִרְאֶה — Abraham names the place 'YHWH-Yireh' (YHWH will see / provide). The covenant name attaches to a place of provision, embedding the God-who-sees in the geography of Israel's memory.
exodus:34:6:chesed: רַב־חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת — 'abounding in חֶסֶד and אֱמֶת.' God's self-revelation to Moses at Sinai. This formula echoes through the Psalms, the Prophets, and into NT confession (Jn 1:14 'full of grace and truth' is its translation).
exodus:34:6:emet: רַב־חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת — paired with חֶסֶד. The two words together name how God can be counted on: loyal in love, firm in truth. Hebrew thought refuses to split steadfastness from loving-action.
leviticus:19:18:ahav: וְאָהַבְתָּ לְרֵעֲךָ כָּמוֹךָ — 'and you shall אָהַב your neighbor as yourself.' Jesus quotes this as the second great commandment (Mk 12:31). אָהַב in Torah is a verb of covenant fidelity, not warm feelings — love that ACTS toward the neighbor's good.
deuteronomy:6:5:ahav: וְאָהַבְתָּ אֵת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ — 'and you shall אָהַב YHWH your God.' The Shema's command. With ALL the heart, soul, and strength — exhaustive, covenant-shaped devotion. The Greatest Commandment (Mk 12:30).
psalms:23:4:tsalmavet: גֵּיא צַלְמָוֶת — 'the valley of צַלְמָוֶת.' Compound of צֵל (shadow) + מָוֶת (death) — 'death-shadow,' the deepest darkness. KJV's 'valley of the shadow of death' captures the dread, though the literal sense is closer to 'deep darkness.'
psalms:23:6:chesed: אַךְ טוֹב וָחֶסֶד יִרְדְּפוּנִי — 'surely goodness and חֶסֶד will pursue me.' The verb is hunting vocabulary (radaph): God's loyal-love HUNTS the believer, doesn't trail behind. The shepherd-psalm closes with chesed as the relentless follower.
psalms:51:11:ruach: וְרוּחַ קָדְשְׁךָ אַל־תִּקַּח מִמֶּנִּי — 'take not your קֹדֶשׁ רוּחַ from me.' One of the few OT references to the Holy Spirit. David, post-Bathsheba, dreads losing the divine presence — the same Spirit that anointed Saul and then departed (1 Sam 16:14).
psalms:100:5:chesed: כִּי־טוֹב יְהוָה לְעוֹלָם חַסְדּוֹ — 'for YHWH is good; his חֶסֶד endures forever.' The refrain that closes Ps 136 (every other verse). חֶסֶד is what makes praise possible: God's covenant loyalty doesn't run out.
proverbs:1:7:yirah: יִרְאַת יְהוָה רֵאשִׁית דָּעַת — 'יִרְאָה of YHWH is the beginning of knowledge.' Not paralyzing fear but reverent awe — the awareness of standing before the One. Wisdom literature's anchor: you can't think rightly about reality without first reckoning with God.
proverbs:3:5:batach: בְּטַח אֶל־יְהוָה בְּכָל־לִבֶּךָ — 'בָּטַח (trust) YHWH with all your heart.' בָּטַח means leaning your full weight on something. Picture trusting a chair to hold you — that bodily commitment, applied to God.
isaiah:6:3:qadosh: קָדוֹשׁ קָדוֹשׁ קָדוֹשׁ — 'holy holy holy.' Hebrew uses repetition for superlative: this triple is the strongest possible קָדוֹשׁ. Isaiah's vision establishes the seraphic praise the NT will echo (Rev 4:8). Holiness = utter set-apartness, otherness.
isaiah:7:14:almah: הָעַלְמָה הָרָה — 'the עַלְמָה will conceive.' Hotly contested: עַלְמָה means a young woman of marriageable age, with no inherent claim about virginity. LXX renders παρθένος (virgin); Matthew 1:23 picks up the LXX reading and ties it to Jesus' birth. Both Hebrew text and Greek translation matter for the verse's reception.
isaiah:53:5:shalom: וּמוּסַר שְׁלוֹמֵנוּ עָלָיו — 'the chastisement of our שָׁלוֹם was upon him.' Wholeness comes through someone else's broken-ness. The Servant absorbs the punishment that secures peace; Christian reading takes this as the gospel's deepest OT articulation.
jeremiah:31:31:berit: הִנֵּה יָמִים בָּאִים וְכָרַתִּי בְּרִית חֲדָשָׁה — 'behold the days are coming when I will cut a בְּרִית חֲדָשָׁה (new covenant).' Jeremiah's promise of internalized Torah, written on the heart. The NT cites this passage more than any other OT text (Heb 8 quotes it in full).
micah:6:8:chesed: וְאַהֲבַת חֶסֶד — 'and love חֶסֶד.' Micah's compact summary of what God requires: justice (מִשְׁפָּט), חֶסֶד, and walking humbly with God. Not three separate items but three lenses on one disposition. חֶסֶד isn't just felt — it's loved, chosen.
micah:6:8:ahav: וְאַהֲבַת חֶסֶד — to אָהַב חֶסֶד is to SET your affection on covenant loyalty. The verb is volitional: not feeling drawn but actively cherishing. Tied to the previous clause: doing justice ALSO requires loving the kind of life it embodies.
